metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the ProvenanceLedger file attribution from `differ.py` to `conflict_resolver.py` (where the class is defined); update harness import path accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan attributes 'ProvenanceLedger' to differ.py ('baseline-seeded via ProvenanceLedger (differ.py)'), but ProvenanceLedger is defined in conflict_resolver.py. differ.py only receives it as a duck-typed parameter and references the class name in docstrings. A harness or monkey-patch that imports ProvenanceLedger from differ would raise ImportError.
findings.location: Signal definitions > Echo suppression: 'baseline-seeded via ProvenanceLedger (differ.py)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(differ.py)' with '(conflict_resolver.py)' in the echo-suppression signal definition; the harness should `from rebar_reconciler.conflict_resolver import ProvenanceLedger`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the blast-radius API attribution from `binding_walk.py` to `classify.py`; update the harness to import `BreakerVerdict` and `census` from `rebar_reconciler.classify`.
findings.finding: The plan attributes `census()['breaker']['allowed']` and `BreakerVerdict.allowed` to `binding_walk.py`, but both `census()` and `BreakerVerdict` are defined in `classify.py`. `binding_walk.py` calls `classify_mod.census()` and `classify_mod.check_blast_radius()` internally but does not export these names. A harness importing `BreakerVerdict` or calling `census()` from `binding_walk` would fail.
findings.location: Signal definitions > Blast-radius breaker: 'assert via the breaker API census()['breaker']['allowed'] == False / BreakerVerdict.allowed (binding_walk.py)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change '(binding_walk.py)' to '(classify.py)' in the blast-radius signal definition; the harness should `from rebar_reconciler.classify import BreakerVerdict, census`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the blast-radius assertion path: either always pass `max_acting_fraction` and `total_bindings` to `census()` (to guarantee the `'breaker'` key is present), or use the unconditional `BindingWalkResult.breaker.allowed` / `BreakerVerdict.allowed` path directly instead.
findings.finding: The blast-radius assertion path `census()['breaker']['allowed'] == False` is only conditionally present: `classify.census()` only adds the `'breaker'` key to its return dict when both `max_acting_fraction` and `total_bindings` are supplied as non-None arguments (classify.py:368–375). The plan cites it as an unconditional assertion path with no mention of these required arguments, so a harness calling `census(decisions)` without them will get a `KeyError` on `['breaker']`.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `census()['breaker']['allowed'] == False` with `walk_result.breaker.allowed == False` (where `walk_result` is the `BindingWalkResult` returned by `compute_binding_walk_mutations`), or if calling `classify.census()` directly, ensure `max_acting_fraction` and `total_bindings` are always supplied.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the module attribution: replace `ProvenanceLedger (differ.py)` with `ProvenanceLedger (conflict_resolver.py)` so the harness imports from the correct owning module.
findings.finding: The echo-suppression signal definition attributes `ProvenanceLedger` to `differ.py` ("baseline-seeded via ProvenanceLedger (differ.py)"), but `ProvenanceLedger` is defined in `conflict_resolver.py` (at line 227), not `differ.py`. `differ.py` is a consumer that accepts a `ProvenanceLedger`-shaped object, but does not define or export the class. A harness implementing the idempotency pre-seeding step that imports `ProvenanceLedger` from `differ.py` will get an `ImportError`.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the plan text to read: "baseline-seeded via ProvenanceLedger (conflict_resolver.py)" and update any harness import to `from rebar_reconciler.conflict_resolver import ProvenanceLedger`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-line note to the C4 signal definition specifying the exact mock injection point the harness must use (e.g., `patch acli_subprocess.time.sleep` + assert `sleep.call_args >= 2.0`, or `JiraAPIError(status_code=429, headers={'Retry-After':'2'})` if C4 lands on `_call_with_retry`) — this must be filled in AFTER reading C4's merged implementation before writing harness code.
findings.finding: The plan's C4 harness asserts `assert >=2s backoff then success` by injecting `429 + Retry-After: 2` into the live retry path, but neither current live path can receive a `Retry-After` header value: `_run_acli` operates on subprocess `CalledProcessError` text output (no HTTP headers accessible), and `_call_with_retry` uses fixed delays `[1, 2, 4]` with no `Retry-After` read (dispatch_one.py:121, 136-137). The ability to inject a `Retry-After: 2` value into the live path and measure >=2s sleep is an unverified capability that depends entirely on what C4 lands — the two paths require completely different mock mechanisms (patching subprocess vs. patching a REST call with a header-bearing exception). The plan's fallback ('if C4 finds Retry-After unavailable, assert jittered-backoff behavior C4 lands') partially mitigates this, but the harness as written cannot be implemented without first knowing C4's exact implementation surface.
findings.location: Signal definitions — C4 (429/Retry-After); the plan's instruction 'monkey-patch the LIVE retry path C4 (lambswool-shaded-eider) implements — the `_run_acli` retry loop / `_call_with_retry`'
findings.suggested_fix: After C4 merges, add a single sentence to the C4 signal definition: 'Harness patches [exact symbol, e.g. `acli_subprocess.time.sleep`] and asserts the first sleep call is >= 2.0s.' This is the only injection point that is path-agnostic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the module attribution for `BreakerVerdict` in the blast-radius signal definition: it is in `classify.py` (`from rebar_reconciler.classify import BreakerVerdict`), not `binding_walk.py`; the harness should assert `result.breaker.allowed == False` on the `BindingWalkResult` or `from rebar_reconciler.classify import BreakerVerdict`.
findings.finding: The plan attributes `BreakerVerdict.allowed` to `binding_walk.py` ('via the breaker API `census()['breaker']['allowed'] == False` / `BreakerVerdict.allowed` (binding_walk.py)'), but `BreakerVerdict` is defined in `classify.py` (line 294), not `binding_walk.py`. `binding_walk.py` imports and uses `classify_mod.check_blast_radius` via injection, and `BindingWalkResult` (in binding_walk.py) holds `breaker: Any`. A harness that does `from binding_walk import BreakerVerdict` will get an `ImportError`.
findings.suggested_fix: Change '`BreakerVerdict.allowed` (binding_walk.py)' to '`BreakerVerdict.allowed` (`classify.py` — `from rebar_reconciler.classify import BreakerVerdict`); accessible on the `BindingWalkResult.breaker` returned by `compute_binding_walk_mutations`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact module-qualified monkey-patch target for C4 (e.g. `acli_subprocess._run_acli` OR `dispatch_one._call_with_retry`) with the rationale, matching whichever path C4 (lambswool-shaded-eider) actually implements backoff in.
findings.finding: The C4 signal definition names two structurally incompatible monkey-patch targets — `_run_acli` retry loop (acli_subprocess.py, subprocess-level, retries on non-zero exit codes) AND `_call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py, application-level, catches JiraAPIError/TimeoutError) — without specifying which one to patch. These live in different modules with different error domains; patching the wrong one produces a no-op test.
findings.suggested_fix: After C4 lands, identify which of the two paths implements the new Retry-After / jitter logic and name it explicitly: e.g. 'monkeypatch `acli_subprocess._run_acli` to raise CalledProcessError with returncode=429 and b"Retry-After: 2" in stderr on the first call; assert sleep >=2s then success.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the echo-suppression signal's module attribution: ProvenanceLedger is defined in `conflict_resolver.py`, not `differ.py`; update the plan text to read 'baseline-seeded via ProvenanceLedger (conflict_resolver.py)'.
findings.finding: The echo-suppression signal says 'baseline-seeded via ProvenanceLedger (differ.py)' but ProvenanceLedger is defined in `conflict_resolver.py` (line 227); `differ.py` only receives it as a parameter. An executor following this citation to seed the baseline will import from the wrong module.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(differ.py)' with '(conflict_resolver.py)' in the echo-suppression signal definition.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the C4 assertion fork before the harness is written: either (a) defer the C4 scenario until lambswool-shaded-eider's probe step is recorded on the ticket, or (b) specify both assertion shapes (Retry-After-present vs absent) as separate conditional sub-cases with explicit test IDs so the harness can implement both and activate the right one at runtime.
findings.finding: The plan contains an unverified capability assertion about C4's deliverable: it asserts the harness can 'assert >=2s backoff then success' against the live `_run_acli`/`_call_with_retry` path, but the C4 ticket (lambswool-shaded-eider) explicitly marks Retry-After availability as UNVERIFIED ('availability is UNVERIFIED') and requires a spike probe as its first step. The validation plan commits to asserting C4 behavior before C4's own probe has confirmed what behavior will be implemented.
findings.location: Signal definitions — C4 (429/Retry-After): 'assert >=2 s backoff then success (or, if C4 finds Retry-After unavailable, assert the jittered-backoff behavior C4 lands)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify both C4 assertion branches concretely (e.g. 'if Retry-After present: assert elapsed >= header_value; else assert elapsed in [jitter_min, jitter_max] range') so neither branch requires a runtime design decision.
findings.finding: The C4 assertion is conditional on what a sibling ticket implements: 'or, if C4 finds Retry-After unavailable, assert the jittered-backoff behavior C4 lands' — the executor cannot pre-write this assertion without knowing C4's outcome, and there is no described mechanism to discover it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a concrete C1 assertion shape (e.g. 'a BRIDGE_ALERT event file with kind=`conflict-both-sides` is written to the ticket dir, OR a comment body matching pattern X is posted') that the harness can assert — or document explicitly that C1's scenario is a deferred stub until C1 ships, with a named TODO marker in the harness.
findings.finding: The C1 conflict signal definition instructs the harness to 'assert against the exact conflict record C1 (feline-wandering-dassierat) lands' and 'reference C1's implemented signal at validation time,' but C1 is `status: open` / `preconditions_summary: pre-manifest` and its AC defines only that 'a conflict record (comment/alert)' fires — no field name, kind string, tag value, event schema key, or observable proof point is defined anywhere. An executor has no concrete vocabulary to write the assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete assertion shape for the C4 Retry-After-unavailable branch (e.g. 'assert time.sleep was called at least once with a value >=1s and <=60s, consistent with jittered exponential backoff') so both branches of the conditional have verifiable pass criteria.
findings.finding: The C4 signal defines two mutually exclusive assertion branches — (a) '>=2s backoff then success' when Retry-After is available, and (b) 'assert the jittered-backoff behavior C4 lands' when Retry-After is unavailable — but branch (b) has no co-located decision rule: no timing measurement method, tolerance, minimum sleep value, or observable proof point is specified for the Retry-After-unavailable case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the teardown retry backoff curve explicitly (e.g., base=1s, multiplier=2, cap=8s) and expose it as a named constant or config parameter so it can be adjusted without code archaeology.
findings.finding: The teardown-failure fallback specifies '3x exponential backoff' but defers the curve definition ('define the curve') without specifying it, leaving the backoff parameters (base delay, multiplier, cap) to be hardcoded at implementation time with no documented spec or configuration hook.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Teardown-failure fallback: each cleanup call retries 3x with exponential backoff (define the curve)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Harness section: 'Backoff curve: base 1 s, multiplier 2×, cap 8 s (constants: TEARDOWN_RETRY_BASE_S, TEARDOWN_RETRY_CAP_S); expose as env-overridable for CI environments with stricter timeouts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the teardown retry backoff curve concretely (e.g., base=1 s, multiplier=2, cap=10 s, ±10% jitter) in the Teardown-failure fallback section and AC 3.
findings.finding: The teardown-failure fallback exponential backoff curve is explicitly deferred ('define the curve') without specifying the base delay, multiplier, max delay, or jitter, leaving the AC incompletely specified and the implementation underspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'define the curve' with: base delay 1 s, multiplier 2×, cap 10 s, ±10% jitter (so retries at ~1 s, ~2 s, ~4 s). Update AC 3 to reference these values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `cancelled<->Done+rebar-status:cancelled` to the status round-trip signal definition to match the full `_STATUS_ANNOTATION_LABELS` enum in outbound_differ.py.
findings.finding: The 'Status round-trip incl. LOSSY labels' signal names only `blocked` and `idea<->IDEA` as the statuses under test, but the codebase's `_STATUS_ANNOTATION_LABELS` map in outbound_differ.py includes `cancelled` as a second lossy status (line 341: `'cancelled': 'rebar-status:cancelled'`), and integration tests cover it. The signal definition's enum is incomplete, leaving `cancelled` round-trip coverage to executor discretion.
findings.location: Signal definitions / Status round-trip incl. LOSSY labels
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the signal to: 'local `blocked` -> In Progress + `rebar-status:blocked` -> inbound restores `blocked`; local `cancelled` -> Done + `rebar-status:cancelled` -> inbound restores `cancelled`; `idea<->IDEA` round-trips; assert no oscillation over N=3 passes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the teardown retry backoff curve explicitly (e.g. base=1s, multiplier=2, cap=30s, +0-1s jitter) — or reference the existing `_call_with_backoff` curve (base=2s, cap=60s, jitter 0-1s) from acli_subprocess.py as the canonical curve.
findings.finding: The teardown-failure fallback specifies 'each cleanup call retries 3x with exponential backoff (define the curve)' — the phrase 'define the curve' is an explicit deferral of the backoff parameters (base delay, multiplier, cap, jitter) to the executor, leaving the implementation underspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(define the curve)' with '(base 1 s, ×2, cap 30 s, +0–1 s jitter — matching _call_with_backoff pattern in acli_subprocess.py)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Describe the mechanism by which the harness reads C1's signal at runtime (e.g. 'import the C1 signal constant from `conflict_signals.py:C1_SIGNAL`' or 'read from `signals-manifest.json` at test start') so the C1 assertion step is fully specified.
findings.finding: The plan states 'reference C1's implemented signal at validation time' as a constraint but provides no described step or mechanism for how the harness dynamically discovers C1's signal shape at runtime; the proving command for C1 cannot be written without this.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a body section (or expand the Harness section) describing what the ADR/design note must record (e.g., teardown pattern, isolation choices, re-run instructions) and where it is stored, so AC item 6's ADR sub-criterion is coverable.
findings.finding: AC item 6 requires 'harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note' but no corresponding work is described anywhere in the plan body (Harness section, Isolation section, or elsewhere), making this an orphan acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, item 6: 'harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a two-sentence 'Design note' subsection to Harness: 'After implementation, record the teardown pattern, credential-injection approach, and re-run procedure in docs/adr/NNN-e2e-reconciler-harness.md. The ADR must be merged before the ticket closes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Harness section the report output spec: file format (JSON or JUnit XML — pick one), the exact output path (e.g., `test-results/reconciler-e2e.xml`), and how it is attached as a CI artifact, so AC item 2 is fully coverable.
findings.finding: AC item 2 requires the harness to 'write a JSON/JUnit report to a named path attached to the ticket', but the plan body (Harness section and all signal definitions) never mentions report generation, format, or output path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, item 2: 'harness exits 0 all-green and writes a JSON/JUnit report to a named path attached to the ticket'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the backoff curve in the plan (e.g. base 1 s × 2^attempt, cap 8 s) so the executor can implement it without a design decision.
findings.finding: The teardown-failure fallback says 'exponential backoff (define the curve)' — this explicitly defers the backoff curve design to the executor, making it a placeholder that blocks writing the retry logic.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(define the curve)' with a concrete spec, e.g. 'base_delay=1 s, multiplier=2, cap=8 s (delays: 1 s, 2 s, 4 s)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the monkey-patch coupling to `_run_acli`/`_call_with_retry` as intentional and document a guard (e.g., assert the target symbol exists before patching, or note that C4 will expose an injectable transport hook the test can use instead).
findings.finding: The C4 signal validation monkey-patches `_run_acli`/`_call_with_retry` — internal acli.py implementation details — without acknowledging this as a brittle coupling to internals or proposing a mitigation (e.g., an injectable retry-transport interface). If C4 renames or refactors these methods, the test harness silently breaks.
findings.location: Signal definitions — C4 (429/Retry-After): 'monkey-patch the LIVE retry path C4 (lambswool-shaded-eider) implements — the `_run_acli` retry loop / `_call_with_retry`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the C4 signal spec: 'Before patching, assert `hasattr(acli_module, "_call_with_retry")` to fail loud if C4 renames it; alternatively, request C4 expose an injectable `_retry_transport` hook the test can swap without symbol-name binding.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the planned inline '3x exp-backoff' teardown retry with delegation to the existing `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (or a thin wrapper around `client.delete_issue` via it), rather than re-implementing the backoff curve inline — cite the existing 20+ call-site pattern as justification.
findings.finding: The teardown-failure fallback specifies a new bespoke '3x exponential-backoff' retry loop ('define the curve') for cleanup calls, but `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` already implements exactly this — retries with [1, 2, 4]s delays, handles JiraAPIError/429/5xx, raises RetryExhaustedError on exhaustion — and has 20+ production call sites in apply_outbound.py, dispatch_one.py, batch_dispatch.py, and apply_inbound.py. Writing a new inline teardown retry loop rebuilds functionality already present in the production codebase.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Teardown-failure fallback: each cleanup call retries 3x with exponential backoff (define the curve)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the teardown fixture, wrap each `client.delete_issue(key)` call in `_call_with_retry(client.delete_issue, key)` (already importable from `dispatch_one` or `batch_dispatch`) and catch `RetryExhaustedError` to trigger the `leaked-artifacts.log` append. This reuses the production retry curve ([1, 2, 4]s, max_retries=3) exactly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the secrets injection mechanism for the Jira API token (e.g., 'CI-native encrypted secret variable' or 'HashiCorp Vault / AWS SSM') so implementors cannot fall back to a committed `.env` file or plaintext CI config.
findings.finding: The plan requires the Jira API token be injected 'via a secret, never logged/committed' but does not name the secrets mechanism (e.g., GitHub Actions encrypted secret, HashiCorp Vault, AWS SSM Parameter Store). Without a named mechanism, implementors may fall back to a local `.env` file or shell-exported variable in a CI script — neither of which guarantees the secret is stored and rotated in a proper secrets manager.
findings.location: Isolation, credentials section — 'token via a secret, never logged/committed'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Isolation, credentials' section: 'Token is injected exclusively via a CI-native encrypted secret variable (e.g., GitHub Actions `secrets.*` or equivalent); never stored in `.env`, shell history, or CI config files.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the exact secrets-injection mechanism for the harness API token (e.g., 'inject via GitHub Actions `${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}`; never pass as a CLI arg or write to disk') so the credential access posture on the Jira boundary-crossing path is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new harness with a Jira API credential on the boundary-crossing path but never names the secrets-management mechanism (e.g., GitHub Actions encrypted secret, environment variable from a vault, CI secret injection). 'Token via a secret, never logged/committed' is stated but the access class of the injection path is undeclared — a concrete mechanism is needed to ensure the token cannot be read by lower-trust actors (e.g., public CI artifact logs, debug output, or subprocess env dumps).
findings.location: Isolation, credentials section — 'token via a secret, never logged/committed'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Isolation/credentials section: 'Token is injected as `${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}` in GitHub Actions (or equivalent CI secret store) and consumed only via the `JIRA_API_TOKEN` environment variable; it is never passed as a CLI argument, written to disk, or included in subprocess debug output.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the teardown spec an explicit step that removes or reverts any bindings-retired.json entries created during the TOMBSTONE/GRACE test scenario (e.g., delete the throwaway env's entire bindings-retired.json, or record and remove only test-injected entries), consistent with guaranteed bilateral cleanup.
findings.finding: The TOMBSTONE/GRACE signal requires N=3 consecutive 404 passes to trigger soft-retirement (writing to bindings-retired.json), but teardown only hard-deletes Jira issues and discards the local env — it does not specify reverting/removing entries written to bindings-retired.json during the test. This is an unreversed destructive write to a persistent artifact.
findings.suggested_fix: Since the local env is a throwaway, clarify that bindings-retired.json lives entirely within the throwaway env directory and is fully discarded with it — or, if shared, add a teardown step: 'remove test binding IDs from bindings-retired.json by ID before discarding the env.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the teardown-failure retry loop reuses `_call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py:100, delays [1,2,4]) or an inline equivalent — reference the existing prior art and justify any deviation rather than deferring curve definition to the executor.
findings.finding: The teardown-failure retry loop (3x exp-backoff for each cleanup call) reinvents logic that already exists in `_call_with_backoff` (acli_subprocess.py:236-292), which implements exponential backoff (2s base, 60s cap, jitter, Retry-After). The plan proposes writing a new inline retry curve without referencing this prior art or explaining why it cannot be reused.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Teardown-failure fallback: each cleanup call retries 3x with exponential backoff (define the curve)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'define the curve' with: 'use delays [1, 2, 4] seconds (matching `_call_with_retry`'s existing curve), no jitter needed for teardown since concurrency is low; on exhaustion append to leaked-artifacts.log'. Alternatively, reuse `_call_with_retry` directly by wrapping `client.delete_issue` in it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access posture of `leaked-artifacts.log` as a CI artifact (e.g., 'artifact visibility is restricted to the CI job; contains only Jira test-project issue IDs, no credentials') or restrict the artifact upload step to internal-only access.
findings.finding: The `leaked-artifacts.log` file is described as 'stored as a CI artifact' but its access posture is undeclared. GitHub Actions CI artifacts are by default accessible to anyone with repository read access, meaning test-project Jira issue IDs (and any identifiers in the log) are publicly readable on public repos or readable by all org members on private repos.
findings.location: Harness — Teardown-failure fallback: 'append the id to leaked-artifacts.log (stored as a CI artifact)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to one harness approach (e.g. 'use pytest autouse fixture as the primary; adapt e2e_validation_probe.sh only if it already covers bilateral teardown') so the executor does not need to perform a suitability investigation.
findings.finding: The harness entry-point decision is a 'verify whether' conditional ('Build on the existing e2e_validation_probe.sh if suitable; otherwise…') that requires the executor to investigate suitability before proceeding.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete report path in the plan (e.g. `ci-artifacts/reconciler-e2e-report.xml`) so CI artifact configuration can be written without clarification.
findings.finding: AC#2 references 'a named path' for the JSON/JUnit report, but no path is actually named anywhere in the plan; CI artifact attachment cannot be configured without it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add e.g. `--junitxml=ci-artifacts/reconciler-e2e-report.xml` to the pytest invocation and reference `ci-artifacts/` as the upload path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete step describing how the teardown-under-failure self-test is structured and invoked (e.g. 'a pytest parametrized test `test_teardown_under_failure` that monkeypatches `create_issue` to raise RuntimeError on the second call, then asserts `leaked-artifacts.log` is empty and both synthetic IDs are absent from Jira').
findings.finding: The teardown-under-failure self-test (RuntimeError injection asserting both artifacts cleaned) is stated only as an AC outcome; no plan step describes how this test is invoked or what the proving command is, leaving it unmeasurable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the C4 signal definition (or Harness section) the exact scoping mechanism for the monkey-patch (e.g., `pytest monkeypatch` fixture or `unittest.mock.patch` context manager) and confirm it is automatically reverted after the C4 assertion, consistent with the isolation guarantee.
findings.finding: The C4 signal requires monkey-patching 'the LIVE retry path' (_run_acli / _call_with_retry) during the test, but the Isolation section guarantees 'ISOLATED throwaway tickets env/branch' without specifying how the monkey-patch is scoped and reversed. If the patch is not confined to a single test's scope (e.g., via a pytest monkeypatch fixture or context manager), it contradicts the isolation guarantee and leaves the live code path modified after the test.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to C4: 'Apply via `unittest.mock.patch` as a context manager (or pytest `monkeypatch` fixture) scoped to the single C4 test function; confirm the original implementation is restored before the next scenario runs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a per-call timeout (e.g., 30 s) for all Jira API calls in the harness (setup, scenario execution, teardown) and specify that timeout expiry raises/surfaces an error rather than hanging.
findings.finding: No timeout is specified for live Jira API calls made during the main test-run phases (setup, scenario execution, assertion); only teardown has retry/fallback logic. A hung or slow Jira response during the main run has no bounded failure mode.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Setup probe', Signal definitions, Isolation/credentials section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that writes to `bindings-retired.json` are atomic (write to a tempfile in the same directory, then `os.replace`/rename) and that the harness isolation guarantees each run uses a separate file path.
findings.finding: The TOMBSTONE/GRACE scenario writes to `bindings-retired.json` but the plan does not address write atomicity (e.g., write-to-tempfile-then-rename) or what happens if the process is interrupted mid-write, risking file corruption if two harness runs overlap or a run is killed.
findings.location: Signal definitions — 'TOMBSTONE/GRACE' scenario; Harness section — isolation
findings.suggested_fix: Use `pathlib` + `tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(dir=target_dir, delete=False)`, write, then `os.replace(tmp, bindings_retired_path)` to make the write atomic on POSIX systems.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
